title: End of module: Trigonometry II ‚ÄĒ Identities, Compound Angles and Equations

questions.text: Given $\sin A = \dfrac{3}{5}$ with $A$ acute, find the exact value of $\cos(2A)$ as a decimal.
questions.explanation: Since $A$ is acute, $\cos A = \sqrt{1-(3/5)^2} = 4/5$. Then $\cos(2A) = 1 - 2\sin^2A = 1 - 2(9/25) = 1 - 18/25 = 7/25 = 0.28$. Check with the other form: $\cos(2A) = 2\cos^2A - 1 = 2(16/25) - 1 = 32/25 - 1 = 7/25$ ‚ÄĒ the two forms agree.

questions.text: If $\tan A = 2$, find the exact value of $\tan(2A)$ as a decimal (3 s.f.).
Using the compound angle formula with $75¬į = 45¬į + 30¬į$, what is the exact value of $\cos(75¬į)$?
Express $5\sin x + 12\cos x$ in the form $R\sin(x+\alpha)$ with $R>0$. What is the exact value of $R$?
For $5\sin x + 12\cos x \equiv R\sin(x+\alpha)$ with $R>0$ and $0¬į<\alpha<90¬į$, find $\alpha$ in degrees to 1 decimal place.
Which of the following are solutions of $\cos x = -\dfrac12$ for $0¬į \le x \le 360¬į$? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: $\cos x = -\tfrac12$ has principal value $x_1 = 120¬į$ (since $\cos^{-1}(-0.5)=120¬į$). Cosine is negative in quadrants 2 and 3, so the two solutions in $[0¬į,360¬į]$ are $x = 120¬į$ and $x = 360¬į-120¬į = 240¬į$. Checking: $\cos(120¬į) = -0.5$ ‚úď and $\cos(240¬į) = -0.5$ ‚úď; the other options do not satisfy the equation.

questions.text: Which of the following are solutions of $\tan x = 1$ for $0¬į \le x \le 360¬į$? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: $\tan x = 1$ has principal value $45¬į$, and $\tan$ has period $180¬į$, so the solutions in $[0¬į,360¬į]$ are $x=45¬į$ and $x=45¬į+180¬į=225¬į$. Checking: $\tan(45¬į)=1$ ‚úď and $\tan(225¬į)=1$ ‚úď (third quadrant, both sine and cosine negative so their ratio is positive); $135¬į$ and $315¬į$ give $\tan x=-1$, not $1$.

questions.text: Which of the following are solutions of $2\sin^2x - \sin x - 1 = 0$ for $0¬į \le x \le 360¬į$? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: Factorising as a quadratic in $\sin x$: $(2\sin x+1)(\sin x - 1)=0$, so $\sin x = 1$ or $\sin x = -\tfrac12$. $\sin x=1$ gives $x=90¬į$ only. $\sin x=-\tfrac12$ has principal value $-30¬į$; since sine is negative in quadrants 3 and 4, the solutions are $180¬į+30¬į=210¬į$ and $360¬į-30¬į=330¬į$. So the full solution set in the interval is $\{90¬į, 210¬į, 330¬į\}$.

questions.text: Solve $\cos(2x) = \sin x$ for $0¬į \le x \le 360¬į$. Give the smallest positive solution in degrees.
questions.explanation: Using $\cos(2x)=1-2\sin^2x$: $1-2\sin^2x = \sin x \implies 2\sin^2x+\sin x-1=0 \implies (2\sin x-1)(\sin x+1)=0$, so $\sin x = \tfrac12$ or $\sin x=-1$. $\sin x=\tfrac12$ gives $x=30¬į$ or $x=150¬į$; $\sin x=-1$ gives $x=270¬į$. The smallest positive solution is $30¬į$ (full solution set: $\{30¬į,150¬į,270¬į\}$).

questions.text: Solve $3\sin x + 4\cos x = 2$ for $0¬į \le x \le 360¬į$. Give the smaller of the two solutions, in degrees to 1 decimal place.
questions.explanation: Write $3\sin x+4\cos x \equiv 5\sin(x+53.1¬į)$ (since $R=\sqrt{3^2+4^2}=5$ and $\alpha=\arctan(4/3)=53.1¬į$). Then $5\sin(x+53.1¬į)=2 \implies \sin(x+53.1¬į)=0.4$. Let $u=x+53.1¬į$, ranging over $[53.1¬į,413.1¬į]$. Solving $\sin u=0.4$: $u=23.6¬į$ (out of range), $u=156.4¬į$, $u=383.6¬į$, both in range. So $x = 156.4¬į-53.1¬į=103.3¬į$ or $x=383.6¬į-53.1¬į=330.4¬į$. The smaller solution is $103.3¬į$.

questions.text: What is the maximum value of $3\sin x + 4\cos x$, and at what value of $x$ (in the range $0¬į$ to $360¬į$) does it occur?
questions.explanation: Writing $3\sin x + 4\cos x \equiv 5\sin(x+53.1¬į)$, the maximum value of $5\sin(x+53.1¬į)$ is $5$ (the amplitude), occurring when $x+53.1¬į = 90¬į$, i.e. $x = 36.9¬į$. Checking: at $x=36.9¬į$, $3\sin(36.9¬į)+4\cos(36.9¬į) \approx 3(0.600)+4(0.800) = 1.8+3.2 = 5.0$ ‚úď.
